evidence: Both reviewed files are small, static contract-definition modules with no imports, init-time mutation, or exported initialization side effects.
Contract identity values are defined as package-level constants/vars and assembled directly into catalog entries, giving a clear and explicit contract registry surface inside a single package.

The StarTram error contract and websocket protocol action contracts live in the same package area and follow different construction forms (helper-based vs inline struct literal), increasing coupling to package-local conventions as new contract families are added.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: Centralization is coherent for now, but the mixed construction patterns across contract families make this package a potential coupling boundary hotspot as protocol surface area grows; a stricter internal contract-builder convention would reduce drift risk.

evidence: Hotspot data in the blind packet shows very high indirection and wide-bag pressure in batch files (e.g., `goseg/broadcast/state.go` max chain depth 5 with 44 chains; `goseg/main.go` and `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` appear in the top wide-param-bag list).

`importer/importer.go`, `startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, and `routines/logstream/logs.go` each layer runtime composition through many callback fields plus option-merging hooks, then call thin wrapper helpers, increasing call depth for common paths.
`defaults/defaults.go` exposes mutable config artifacts that are used as shared default providers by downstream runtime constructors.
`docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` and `docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go` repeatedly compose and merge fine-grained operation bags rather than expressing a clearer boundary contract.
abstraction_fitness.issues_preventing_higher_score: High indirection density across runtime-seam wiring and pervasive callback-option patterns still add meaningful complexity; these abstractions are not consistently tied to reusable domain behavior and frequently look like testability scaffolding rather than policy/translation boundaries.

evidence: `README.md` lists explicit contract-focused gates in `.github/workflows/upload-contract-gate.yml` and ` .github/scripts/check-upload-contract.sh`, reducing the chance of silent contract-regression for upload/C2C/StarTram families.
The same README links deterministic contract tests (`protocol_contracts_test.go`, `startram_contracts_test.go`, `contracts_test.go`) for contract-definition regression paths.

The two contract surfaces under review are small, declarative files (`protocol_contracts.go`, `startram_contracts.go`), making them suitable for table-driven contract tests rather than broad integration coverage in this same module.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: The coverage policy is strong but currently documented in README-driven process; keeping this as an executable policy file in-repo near contract surfaces would reduce reliance on human execution and onboarding interpretation.

evidence: Transition-builder functions in urbit workflow capture mutable ship config once during step construction, then execute logic later in closures (`buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps`, `buildToggleDevModeSteps`, `buildRebuildContainerSteps`), which is a fragile control-plane pattern when runtime state can change between build and execution.

Power/network toggle handlers and transition builders duplicate the same lifecycle flow (status read -> config section persist -> cleanup/start logic) across files instead of a shared transition coordinator.

The transition path mixes transition-routing glue with runtime mutation and restart side-effects in several small functions, creating repeated branching logic that is easy to drift across similar operations.

Importer and cleanup code paths silently drop I/O failure signals in some branches (e.g., temp file/archive cleanup in `goseg/importer/importer.go`) while similar operations in other branches return wrapped errors.

Wi-Fi parser helpers have diverging error contracts for similar malformed-input scenarios (`parseNmcliWifiDeviceRecords` returns an error, while `parseNmcliWifiScanResults` discards parsing issues and returns partial data).
Several resource cleanup calls in Docker/network paths use deferred `Close()` without handling returned errors, producing hidden resource-release failures and no caller visibility.
error_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: ['Normalize error return behavior for similarly scoped failures across importer, parser, and runtime/network helpers.', 'Require cleanup/close failure handling via explicit reporting or explicit policy (must-fail vs best-effort) and document it.']

findings.summary: Exported mutable defaults allow cross-run or cross-package mutation of baseline config and runtime behavior.
evidence: `defaults/defaults.go` defines shared defaults as package-level variables (e.g., `UrbitConfig`, `NetdataConfig`, `WgConfig`) rather than immutable creators.

Downstream code paths merge against shared defaults through seam-style constructors (not unique per caller), so any external mutation of those defaults can alter subsequent configuration behavior globally.
Several dependent files in this batch consume these defaults indirectly when assembling runtime/config flows, amplifying blast radius if baseline state is changed.
findings.suggestion: Replace exported mutable defaults with unexported constants/templates plus constructor functions that return deep copies per call; keep `goseg/defaults` immutable and expose `func DefaultUrbitConfig() structs.UrbitConfig` style accessors that defensively copy slices/maps. Update call sites to use these getters rather than referencing package variables.

findings.summary: Runtime seam composition is split into many callback-only layers with limited policy/value translation.
evidence: `importer/importer.go` defines a large callback container struct and emits many `err*Missing` runtime dependency errors, indicating constructor-time plumbing over behavior-driven seams.
`startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` defines multiple nested runtime structs and option-merging layers, yet many path functions are thin pass-through orchestrators.
`docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`/`runtime_core.go` show repeated operation-bag composition with numerous small merge helpers, creating deep call chains before real logic executes.

Broadcast and logstream files (`broadcast/state.go`, `broadcast/loop.go`, `routines/logstream/logs.go`) follow the same pattern: runtime struct + merged overrides + short wrapper funcs, with limited local policy added at each layer.
findings.suggestion: Introduce narrower, behavior-specific interfaces for each subsystem boundary and pass only needed collaborators instead of broad runtime bags; collapse one-level pass-through wrappers where policy is unchanged, and keep seam layers only where translation, policy, or test isolation is explicitly needed.

findings.summary: Captured ship config snapshots are used later inside transition closures, risking decisions on stale state
evidence: `buildUrbitDomainSteps`, `buildMinIODomainSteps`, `buildRebuildContainerSteps`, and `buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps` read ship config into local variables before returning closures that execute transition actions later.
Closures then branch on captured values (`shipConf.BootStatus`, `currentConf` from the builder path) instead of refreshing state at execution time.
`runTogglePowerTransition` and `runToggleNetworkTransition` in handlers already re-read status/config, showing execution-time reads are possible and needed in this workflow.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a runtime transition context object that reads current ship/runtime state inside each `Run` function (or via a single helper called at execution time), and make step builders pass only immutable inputs (patp/payload/action metadata) to remove stale snapshots as a dependency.

findings.summary: Power/network toggle flows duplicate lifecycle orchestration logic instead of using one transition coordinator
evidence: `runTogglePowerTransition` and `runToggleNetworkTransition` both perform repeated inline sequences: read ship status/config, persist section updates, optional desired-state updates, cleanup, and conditional container start/stop.

The operations builders in `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` re-wrap these same lifecycle motifs (`buildTogglePowerSteps`, `buildToggleDevModeSteps`, `buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps`) with additional closure-specific branches.
New transition types must currently duplicate this orchestration by copy/paste across both handler and operations files, increasing risk of behavioral drift.
findings.suggestion: Extract a small `runUrbitTransition` coordinator API that standardizes: resolve current state, apply section mutation callback, optional desired-state updates, optional cleanup, and restart policy; update `runTogglePowerTransition`, `runToggleNetworkTransition`, and toggle step builders to consume it.

findings.summary: Importer silently drops temp-file removal failures when copy/close operations fail, which can hide partial-import state corruption.
evidence: In `goseg/importer/importer.go` around lines 658-665, the code path that handles `io.CopyBuffer`/close errors invokes `os.Remove(tempFilePath)` without checking its return value.
Because these branches return early on import failure, unobserved temp-file cleanup failures can accumulate stale work directories/files without surfacing to callers.
findings.suggestion: Capture and propagate remove errors in the same branch (or aggregate with existing import error). Prefer a helper like `defer cleanupTemp(tempFilePath)` plus `errors.Join` so callers can detect cleanup regressions under failure conditions.

findings.summary: Archive removal is treated as best-effort in success path, creating inconsistency with other critical error paths and potentially leaving inconsistent import state.
evidence: At `goseg/importer/importer.go` lines 1370-1373, `os.Remove(ctx.ArchivePath)` errors are only logged and do not affect function return status.
The same operation often participates in an import lifecycle where callers treat returned errors as gating conditions, so silently ignoring cleanup failures creates mixed failure semantics.
findings.suggestion: Define explicit cleanup policy for importer lifecycle: either (a) make cleanup failures fatal and return wrapped error, or (b) classify as best-effort but still emit structured diagnostic that callers/tests assert. Implement consistently across all cleanup points.

findings.summary: Docker client close errors are ignored in multiple runtime paths, reducing observability of resource-release failures.
evidence: In `goseg/docker/network/network.go`, several functions defer `cli.Close()` without checking returned error (including lines 47, 80, 99, 116, 132, and 154+).
No call-site-level compensation or logging appears for close failures, so callers cannot distinguish operational success from teardown failure in these branches.
findings.suggestion: Replace bare `defer cli.Close()` with deferred wrappers that capture and either return close errors when primary operation succeeded or aggregate them with `errors.Join`; consider a shared helper to standardize close handling.

findings.summary: Wi-Fi parsing functions in the same module have divergent behavior for malformed input, leading to inconsistent upstream recovery behavior.
evidence: `parseNmcliWifiDeviceRecords` (around lines 108-129) preserves parse errors and returns an error when no valid device records are parsed.
`parseNmcliWifiScanResults` (around lines 132-149) logs parse issues but continues, returning partial scan records without surfacing an error.
`parseNmcliWifiSSIDs` (around lines 201-207) then discards parser error context by returning only SSIDs.
findings.suggestion: Unify parser semantics by making both scanners return `(result, err)` consistently; introduce explicit parse error modes (strict vs tolerant) and use one policy across callers in `wifi_runtime_api.go`.

findings.summary: Startup configuration initialization mutates shared global state and persists it directly, creating hidden cross-module coupling and non-deterministic startup effects.
evidence: `initializeConfig` mutates package-level configuration state during bootstrap rather than returning an owned immutable config object.
Persistence calls are embedded in initialization flow, so imports of config package can trigger writes as a side effect.
Downstream modules read mutated globals, which makes behavior depend on call order and initialization history.
findings.suggestion: Refactor configuration bootstrapping to return a constructed config value from pure functions, inject it into consumers, and isolate persistence behind an explicit repository interface executed only by the composition root.

findings.summary: Router command execution relies on string formatting as the command abstraction boundary, making argument escaping/validation a caller responsibility and inhibiting secure reuse.
evidence: Router flow builds command strings through format helpers and executes them through generic shell dispatch (`formatShellCommand`-style concatenation then command execution).
Command inputs are derived from runtime config/parameters and interpolated into shell text without a typed command/argument abstraction.
No centralized guard exists to enforce quoting or command allowlists for these execution paths.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a command/executor abstraction with typed arguments and explicit validation/escaping at the boundary, and replace direct string interpolation calls in accesspoint lifecycle/router code with that abstraction.

findings.summary: Container status and transition code conflates absence, stopped, and retrieval failures, weakening error signaling and making recovery logic harder to reason about.
evidence: Status lookups focus on running containers and default to fallback tags/values when metadata is incomplete.
Transition/state update paths do not preserve enough distinction between 'container missing', 'container stopped', and transport/API error.
Downstream transitions can therefore treat multiple failure modes as equivalent.
This masks actionable causes and increases incorrect state transitions under partial failures.
findings.suggestion: Define and propagate explicit error variants for not-found/stopped/fetch-failed states, and require transition logic to branch on those categories instead of defaulting silently.

findings.summary: Default event runtime is effectively shared mutable infrastructure, creating implicit coupling across broadcast/lifecycle modules through shared bus state.
evidence: Runtime/default construction caches a package-level singleton via one-time initialization and exposes mutable publish/subscribe channels.
Modules that call constructors with different runtime expectations may inadvertently share or mutate the same underlying event channels.
The pattern mixes global behavior with per-component lifecycle expectations.
findings.suggestion: Replace singleton/default event runtime with an explicit factory that returns independent event runtimes; inject a shared bus only where intentional, via an interface contract.

findings.summary: Polling/state loops and collectors depend directly on real time, process, and transport behavior, making deterministic unit tests difficult and brittle.
evidence: Loops and transitions are driven by concrete timed sleep/retry behavior plus external runtime calls without injectable fakes.
No explicit clock/dependency interface is present in these paths, so tests can’t control timing or externally induced transitions.
Collector/lifecycle modules therefore require broad integration-style setup to exercise common error/state paths.
findings.suggestion: Inject clock and runtime client interfaces into pollers/loops, and extract state transition policies into deterministic pure functions with table-driven tests.
